title: Choosing Tool vs Resource vs Prompt
concept: A practical decision guide for which primitive fits a given integration need.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we learned about 'Capability Discovery'. What does this process allow an LLM client to do?

options.text: Hardcoding all capabilities directly into the LLM weights
Querying the server's listed capabilities so the client knows what is available
Encrypting the prompt templates so the user cannot read them

exercises.explain: Capability discovery allows a client to query the server using a handshake protocol to find out what prompts, resources, and tools are available.

cards.title: Which Tool for the Job?
cards.body: Now that we know how to list all capabilities, how do we decide when to write a Prompt, share a Resource, or build a Tool? Making the right choice keeps your LLM app safe and efficient.

cards.title: The Three-Way Decision Guide
cards.body: To build clean LLM applications, map static templates to Prompts, data-reading endpoints to Resources, and action-taking operations to Tools. choosing the wrong primitive makes your architecture messy and hard to secure.

cards.title: The Kitchen Blueprint
cards.body: Think of a Prompt as a template recipe, a Resource as a fixed ingredient in the pantry, and a Tool as a kitchen appliance like a blender that actually processes inputs and changes things.

cards.title: Shopping Assistant Architecture
cards.body: If your LLM needs to read a database of product prices, expose it as a Resource. If the LLM needs to place an order and charge a credit card, implement that as a Tool.

cards.title: The Danger of 'Tool-Only' Systems
cards.body: You might think you can just make everything a Tool so the LLM has maximum power. However, giving an LLM tool access to read-only data exposes your system to unnecessary side-effects and safety risks.

cards.title: Resource vs Tool
cards.body: Select your architectural primitive based on whether it is a template, a dataset, or an active operation.

left.text: Read-only context like files or database tables.

right.text: Read-write action that changes the system state.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following integration needs is best modeled as a Tool?

options.text: A function that deletes a user account
A list of historical weather data

exercises.explain: Tools are designed for actions that modify the system or external world, such as sending emails, writing files, or updating databases.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If you need to supply a changing database of live inventory to your LLM, a Prompt template is the best primitive to choose.
exercises.explain: Prompts act as templates or guidelines, but they do not dynamically fetch external datasets or perform actions on their own.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: To let an LLM safely read your read-only product catalog without taking actions, you should register it as a...
exercises.sentence: You should register a read-only product catalog as a Resource.
exercises.explain: A product catalog is read-only information, so it should be exposed as a Resource rather than an active Tool.

exercises.prompt: Which design suggestion sounds like it was written by an AI hallucinating an over-complicated architectural pattern?

options.text: We need to expose our company wiki to the AI. Since it's read-only document search, let's expose it as a Resource.
To implement the user lookup, we will define a schema with <tool_call_schema> tag using write-back permissions to read the database.
For our customer service bot, we'll write a Prompt template for tone guidelines, and a Tool to process refunds.

exercises.explain: Option B is clearly written by an AI because it creates a fake, overly elaborate XML tool schema for a simple read-only data lookup, which should instead be a Resource.

exercises.prompt: You are building a Calendar integration. You need to: 1) Provide a reusable email template for meeting minutes, 2) Let the LLM view the user's agenda for today, and 3) Let the LLM schedule a new meeting. How should you model these?

options.text: Tool for the template, Resource for adding the event, Prompt for reading the agenda
Prompt for the template, Resource for reading the agenda, Tool for adding the event
Resource for the template, Tool for reading the agenda, Prompt for adding the event
Tool for all three since they all happen inside a calendar app

exercises.explain: Creating a calendar event is an action that modifies state in the real world, which makes it a Tool. Reading the user's existing agenda is fetching static/dynamic context, which is a Resource. Offering a template for a meeting recap is a Prompt.
